data.title: Dutch minister admits being blindsided by China’s retaliation in Nexperia chip crisis
data.description:
Export controls may have been the economic weapon of choice in 2025, but the Dutch minister at the centre of the Nexperia crisis has admitted he was blindsided by Beijing when it blocked the company’s chips from leaving China.
His concession raises fresh questions over how much planning went into the decision to reach for a Cold War-era law to intervene in the Chinese-owned, Dutch-headquartered chipmaker in September, a move that triggered a supply chain shock in the global automotive industry.
“An assessment was made of possible counterreactions; this wasn’t the most likely reaction from China,” said Vincent Karremans, the Dutch economy minister, during a debate on the Nexperia saga in the House of Representatives on Thursday evening.
On October 4, five days after Karremans triggered the Goods Availability Act to prevent Nexperia’s Chinese owners from transferring production capacity from Europe to China, Beijing restricted the shipment of finished chips from the company’s back-end plants in Dongguan.
It is there that 70 per cent of its estimated 10 billion legacy chips are tested and packaged each year – an obvious chokepoint at a time when they have been mercilessly exploited by major superpowers.


During a long hearing, Karremans – whose behaviour was described variously as “reckless”, “sloppy” and “amateurish” – was quizzed on why he did not predict Beijing’s response, which caused some global auto giants to idle production lines due to a shortage of chips.
A defensive Karremans described a “well-considered and substantiated decision, in which we took all the risks and all the information into account and carefully weighed them against each other”.
“This wasn’t a likely option, because an export control measure is a non-proliferation instrument,” he said.
While some appreciated the dilemma facing the Dutch minister, many demanded clarity on why he moved in the way he did, without consulting parliament, the European Union or the automotive industry.
“We understand the need to intervene. But we do question the sloppy way in which it was done, like a driver accelerating but forgetting to update the navigation system,” said Laurens Dassen, a centre-left lawmaker with Volt Netherlands.
“Did the minister properly anticipate China’s potential reaction? Why were we, as parliament, only informed on October 14, when China had already announced on October 4 that it would not export chips?” he asked.
Karremans responded that “you never know how China will react ... there’s no certainty about that in advance. When you make such a decision, you never have any certainty about how it will unfold”.
“You never consider a scenario in isolation, but always in relation to a certain degree of probability with which you think it will occur. That’s how that assessment was made.”
The minister was forced to deny claims from several lawmakers that a planned trip to China was cancelled because ministers in Beijing refused to see him.
“Why isn’t the minister going to China next week? Because the Chinese ministers can’t be there? Isn’t that strange? You plan something like that, don’t you? You make an appointment with someone, don’t you?” demanded Alexander Kops of the right-wing Party for Freedom.
Karremans said “this was not the case”, adding that he had personally decided to postpone the trip.
“You always check … whether you can also meet at the ministerial level … and then it often doesn’t fit into the schedule. Ministers … have all sorts of obligations,” Karremans explained.

The minister further defended his dramatic intervention, saying he personally had seen “very, very convincing” evidence that Nexperia’s Chinese owner, Zhang Xuezheng, was transferring funds, technology and confidential knowledge to a separate foreign entity he controlled; preparing to move patent rights out of Europe and planning to downsize or relocate European production.
“It would have happened faster anyway if I hadn’t intervened, because then they would have been gone in the foreseeable future.”
Asked by lawmakers to clarify the role of the United States, Karremans denied any link between Washington’s threats to add Nexperia to a government blacklist and its insistence on removing the Chinese management – outlined in independent court documents – and his move to impose the act on September 30, the date the US blacklist was expanded.
“There’s been a lot of speculation about that … what was the US’ role in this? Did the US call the Netherlands, and was there pressure to issue this order? I can tell you … that’s not the case,” he insisted.
Karremans confirmed that the EU was not consulted in advance of his decision because he was worried about leaks, which could have sparked a scramble to remove the technology from Europe.
“Why didn’t I inform European countries beforehand? I considered it, but didn’t. I’ll explain why. With these kinds of decisions, you keep the circle very small, especially when it concerns national economic security,” Karremans said.
“If the intention to make this decision became public, it could have had serious consequences. If the acting party, in this case the CEO in question, had learned of what we were about to do, irreversible steps could have been taken quickly.”
With the dust still far from settled, lawmakers pressed Karremans on what happens now: whether China will resume full shipments, whether Europe can restore trust in his decision-making, and whether the diplomatic damage with Beijing can be repaired.
The minister talked up his consultations with China, saying that he had suspended the order “as a diplomatic gesture” to Beijing, “as they had taken the first step towards us” in permitting the flow of chips from Nexperia’s Dongguan plant.
“If that gesture from China hadn’t happened, I wouldn’t have done it,” said Karremans, although he said that the two corporate entities involved – Nexperia’s Dutch and Chinese operations – remain at loggerheads.
“Due to everything that has happened, contact is currently very limited. It is therefore crucial that we pursue a pragmatic solution that fully restores both parts of the value chain,” Karremans said, adding that “contact is still suboptimal”.
While the minister defended his actions in broad terms, he admitted regret over an interview in the Guardian last month, in which he stated he would take exactly the same course of action if he were in the same position again. It is understood that the article in question led several European governments to complain to The Hague.
“In retrospect, I can say with absolute certainty that I could have handled things differently … especially when it concerns a quote in the press,” Karremans said.
Pieter Grinwis, an opposition conservative MP, said the “infamous” interview had “added fuel to the fire”.

data.title: Legco election hopefuls scale back campaigning in Hong Kong fire aftermath
data.description:
Candidates started campaigning again this week for Hong Kong’s coming Legislative Council election, but opted for a more subdued approach, including fewer street booths and volunteers, in the aftermath of last week’s deadly fire in Tai Po.
Some Legco hopefuls also unveiled new policy platforms in the run-up to Sunday’s poll, such as calling for a thorough investigation into the fire and enhanced building safety oversight.
As of Friday, two major political parties also said they were facing electoral challenges.
The poll will be the first major citywide event to be held after the inferno raged through seven of the eight blocks at Wang Fuk Court last week, killing 159 people and injuring another 79.
Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu announced on Tuesday that he would hold the election as planned, stressing the importance of selecting new lawmakers who would partner with him to make reforms following the tragedy.
Candidates opted to quietly resume their campaigns following the fire, with fewer street booths and volunteers deployed.
Election hopefuls also avoided using loudspeakers to attract supporters and proclaim their political platform.
“Less noise, fewer volunteers and fewer stations. It is a sign of my respect for the fire victims,” Hong Kong Island West constituency contender Jeremy Young Chit-on said.




The district councillor is competing against incumbent lawmakers Judy Chan Kapui, Chan Hok-fung and Kwok Wai-keung and another district councillor, Wong Chau-ping, to win one of two seats in the area.
On Friday, Young only brought along two volunteers to campaign on the street, but said the response from residents had been warm.
A Post reporter visited Hennessy Road at noon that day. The area is typically a hub for Hong Kong Island East candidates, but there was sparse campaign activity between Wan Chai and Causeway Bay, with only scattered banners and no street booths.
The constituency encompasses the Eastern and Wan Chai districts.
Subdued campaigning was also observed in the New Territories South West constituency, which covers Kwai Tsing and Tsuen Wan, with candidates’ usual loudspeakers notably absent.
Following last week’s fire, Kwok Fu-yung, a candidate for the area, updated her campaign slogan to emphasise “strengthening supervision of building renovation and safeguarding residents’ safety”.
Fellow contender Marcella Cheung Man-ka also modified her platform to include the call to “investigate thoroughly [the fire] and monitor until the end”.
Kwok and Cheung are competing against Lo Yuen-ting, Joephy Chan Wing-yan and Mok Yee-ki for seats.
The Democratic Alliance for the Betterment and Progress of Hong Kong (DAB) and the New People’s Party also made urgent appeals to voters ahead of Sunday’s election.
DAB chairman Gary Chan Hak-kan described his party’s situation as “extremely critical” in a video message on the group’s social media page.
He claimed some individuals were using the fire incident to discredit the DAB, referring to online criticism against the party after member Peggy Wong Pik-kiu faced public scrutiny in connection with the fire for her past work as a consultant to the estate’s incorporated owners.
“I urgently need your vote to implement reform proposals in Legco … I appeal to all Hong Kong residents to come out to vote and steadfastly support the DAB,” Chan said.
The party is fielding 26 candidates, including Chan himself, who is contesting the New Territories North East seat alongside Dominic Lee Tsz-king, Allan Wong Wing-ho, Ku Wai-ping and Calvin Tang Siu-fung.

The New People’s Party, which is fielding eight candidates, described the election struggle as “challenging”.
“The New People’s Party is a rational and pragmatic voice that is indispensable for Hong Kong, and calls for families to mobilise their votes,” it said in a statement.
Sunday’s Legco poll will be the second since Beijing overhauled the electoral system to ensure only patriots hold power.
The election will see 161 candidates competing for 90 seats.
Among them, 51 are competing in the geographical constituencies for 20 seats, while 60 are standing in the 28 functional constituencies for 30 seats.
The remaining 50 candidates will contest the 40 seats in the Election Committee constituency.
Residents can vote for their representatives in the geographical constituencies, while lawmakers in the functional constituencies and the Election Committee constituency are chosen by a small circle of voters in their respective sectors.
For the first time in two decades, no individual or organisation has applied to conduct exit polls for the coming election, according to the government’s official election website.
In the 2021 poll, the Hong Kong Research Association and i-Cable News Limited were approved to carry out the surveys.
Thomas So Chi-ki, chairman of the association, said his group, which has conducted exit polls for more than 10 years, had decided to opt out after careful consideration of manpower, resources and candidates’ backgrounds.
Click here for the full list of Legco election candidates.
Additional reporting by Matthew Cheng

data.title: Hong Kong Squash Open: Japan’s Watanabe summons grit to shock teenager Orfi
data.description:
Fourteen years after she left her family in Japan aged 12 to pursue her squash dreams, Satomi Watanabe stunned Amina Orfi on Friday to reach her first platinum tournament semi-final at the Milwaukee Hong Kong Squash Open.
Watanabe, the world No 7, raced into a 2-0 lead, but she appeared destined for disappointment after Egyptian rising star Orfi rattled off the next two games. The 26-year-old, who recently began working with a sports psychologist, gathered herself and overcame a final-game warning for dissent to clinch it 11-4 and prevail 3-2.
Only the second player, after Hania El Hammamy, to beat Orfi, the world No 3, this season, Watanabe gleefully tossed her racquet skywards and released a jubilant roar.
The 26-year-old will play Olivia Weaver in Saturday’s semi-finals, after the American overcame Tinne Gilis of Belgium 3-0.

“I tried to forget the fourth game and focus on my target, which helped me recover my concentration,” Watanabe said.
“Sports psychology definitely helps me keep my composure in those situations. Anything can happen on court, and I mentally prepare for every eventuality because I get panicked if I don’t have the answers straight away.”
Watanabe moved to Malaysia as a schoolgirl because of the lack of access to squash in Japan.
“When I said I played squash, people didn’t know what it was,” she said.
“I’m really grateful my parents let me leave the country to pursue my dream; it didn’t feel a sacrifice to me and it was worth it.”
Now based in the UK, after gaining a 2:1 sports and exercise science degree from the University of Roehampton, Watanabe said that becoming the first Japanese player of either gender to reach the world’s top 10 in March last year had given her “confidence but also the pressure of knowing I have people chasing me”.
“I am now looking at who is at the top and trying to chase them,” Watanabe said.
The World Games gold medallist this year, Watanabe hopes her increasing profile is “the first step to more people playing and coming to watch squash” in her homeland.
Japan staged an event on the lower-level Challenger Tour in October. “To have a bigger tournament that I could play would be great,” Watanabe said.
“Recognition of squash in Japan is growing. More people know it’s a sport and some are playing.”
Watanabe lost to Orfi in last month’s China Open final, after she had beaten El Hammamy in the last four.
“I had mental and physical fatigue after the best win of my career in the semi-final,” she said. “It means a lot to beat Amina, she’s one of the best players on tour. I was emotional towards the end because I wanted to win so much.”
The 2023 men’s champion, Paul Coll, ruthlessly dispatched Marwan Elshorbagy 3-0 to advance to the semi-finals.
After being mobbed for autographs and pictures following his 29-minute victory, New Zealander Coll said: “The people here seem to love squash and love us, they give us lots of support and want signatures and photos. It’s awesome and why I like coming to Hong Kong.
“We don’t get it everywhere. The passion here for squash is pretty cool.”

data.title: Hong Kong actress Cecilia Cheung accuses former agent of HK$8.8 million fraud
data.description:
Hong Kong actress Cecilia Cheung Pak-chi has accused her former agent of exerting undue pressure on her and defrauding her out of 8 million yuan (US$1.13 million) by making false representations.
Cheung, 45, took the witness box at the High Court on Friday to counter allegations that she reneged on a promise to Asia Entertainment Group (AEG) president Samuel Yu Yuk-hing to appear in four films produced by the now liquidated company between 2011 and 2019.
“Throughout my life, I, Cecilia Cheung, have hated having to explain everything to others. I don’t care how anybody tries to vilify me. I just gulp it down,” Cheung said while appearing to fight back her tears.
Yu, who claimed to be Cheung’s god-grandfather, alleged that the actress breached her contractual duties after their relationship turned sour in 2014.
The litigant earlier told the court that the artist had been ungrateful and had refused to return the favour after he offered her a HK$40 million (US$5.1 million) deal in July 2011 to help her overcome financial difficulties.
He insisted that Cheung had entered into a personal contract with him rather than with AEG, meaning she remained bound by the agreement despite the company’s liquidation in August 2013.
Defence lawyers accused Yu of forging the contract while arguing that Cheung had not breached a separate agreement to perform in two additional films because they were never produced.

Cheung said on Friday that she urgently needed HK$40 million in July 2011 to complete a HK$128 million purchase of a flat and parking space at Century Tower in the Mid-Levels.
She confirmed that she had sought Yu’s help through her assistant, but maintained that the genuine contract with AEG contained her fingerprint, whereas the one submitted by the plaintiff did not.
Cheung also criticised her former agent for allegedly forcing her to “cooperate” with his projects and threatening to spread false rumours about her if she refused.
The actress claimed Yu defrauded her of 8 million yuan after she agreed to appear on the mainland Chinese reality show Muse Dress in 2014.
Cheung said Yu initially told her she would receive 7 million yuan for the appearance, before her assistant discovered that the television station had actually offered 15 million yuan for her services.
She further accused Yu of lying about his need for quick cash to treat his cancer when confronted about the discrepancy.
The actress said she decided to remain silent over the incident, fearing she might lose the job.
In July 2014, she instructed her lawyers to issue a letter to AEG indicating her intention to terminate their contract, but asked them to soften the wording to avoid angering the plaintiff.
Cheung is expected to continue giving evidence on Monday.
She became a household name after being cast as a young nightclub hostess in Chow’s hit film, King of Comedy, in 1999. She won best actress at the 23rd Hong Kong Film Awards for her role in Lost in Time, which aired in 2003.
She married singer Nicholas Tse Ting-fung in 2006, but the ­couple divorced in 2011. She has three sons.

data.title: PLA holds urban warfare drill as Taipei looks to boost ‘asymmetric capabilities’
data.description:
The People’s Liberation Army has carried out an urban warfare drill, according to state media, in what appears to be a response to Taipei’s bid to fund improved “asymmetric capabilities”.
In a report on Wednesday, state broadcaster CCTV aired footage of troops carrying out an operation to seize a built-up area in a war-torn city.
The drill – involving reconnaissance, clearing and occupation – was part of a live-fire exercise conducted by a PLA Ground Force brigade that aimed to “test multiple combat skills that are essential for urban offensive operations”, according to the report.
The infantry unit was shown moving into an area of concrete ruins in a light protected tactical vehicle and taking over a target building deemed critical to the city’s infrastructure.
Armed with rifles and portable anti-tank rockets, the soldiers were shown manoeuvring past obstacles including burning barricades on the road and barbed wire. The vehicle had a heavy machine gun mounted on it and a reconnaissance turret.

While the report did not say when or where the exercise was conducted, it was aired soon after Taiwanese leader William Lai Ching-te proposed a US$40 billion “supplementary defence budget” for the self-ruled island last week.
Writing in The Washington Post on November 25, Lai said: “This landmark package will not only fund significant new arms acquisitions from the United States, but also vastly enhance Taiwan’s asymmetrical capabilities.
“In doing so, we aim to bolster deterrence by inserting greater costs and uncertainties into Beijing’s decision-making on the use of force.”
However, Lai’s proposal was blocked by Taiwan’s opposition lawmakers earlier this week.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China, to be reunited by force if necessary. It has ramped up pressure on Taiwan since independence-leaning Lai took office last year.
Most countries – including the US, its main defence backer – do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state. However, Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the island by force and is legally bound to supply Taiwan with weapons to defend itself.
The PLA has conducted similar training and drills in recent years in preparation for a potential conflict across the Taiwan Strait.
It has also built structures that resemble Taiwan’s key government buildings at a military base in Inner Mongolia, according to satellite images from earlier this year.
In April, the PLA Eastern Theatre Command held a drill dubbed Strait Thunder-2025A that involved simulated precision attacks on a liquid natural gas terminal that mirrored a key LNG receiving station at the port in Kaohsiung, on the southern coast of Taiwan.
And footage of a live-fire drill at a PLA military base in 2015, which was also aired on CCTV, showed soldiers attacking a structure that looked similar to the five-storey presidential office in Taipei.

As the PLA’s military might has rapidly expanded in recent years, Washington has focused on strengthening Taiwan’s asymmetric warfare capabilities – also called the “porcupine strategy”.
That strategy has seen arms sale priorities shifting from large conventional platforms to small, mobile, lethal weapons with high survivability – like portable Javelin anti-tank missiles and Stinger air defence missiles, as well as small drones like the Switchblade loitering munition system.
Taiwan’s military is also trying to improve training and readiness for urban warfare. This year’s Han Kuang – the island’s largest annual exercises – included urban survival drills for the first time that were conducted under what the military called “unprecedented” live-fire conditions to increase the “whole-of-society resilience”.

data.title: Major Chinese airlines extend full refund offers for Japan-bound flights by 3 months
data.description:
Major Chinese airlines have extended their offer of full refunds for flights to Japan for another three months, as the heated diplomatic row between Beijing and Tokyo shows no signs of easing.
On Friday, Air China, China Eastern Airlines and China Southern Airlines – the country’s largest state-owned carriers – along with several others, including Hainan Airlines, Shandong Airlines, Spring Airlines and Xiamen Airlines, issued statements allowing free ticket refunds or itinerary changes for flights booked through March 28 next year.
The policies followed Chinese carriers’ announcement last month allowing free ticket refunds or itinerary changes for flights booked until December 31. That announcement came hours after Beijing issued a travel warning on November 14, urging Chinese citizens to avoid visiting Japan, citing safety issues.
More than 1,900 flights from China to Japan scheduled for this month have been cancelled, Chinese state broadcaster CCTV reported on Monday, citing data from online platforms.
The figure represented more than 40 per cent of the flights from mainland China to Japan scheduled for December, according to CCTV.

For years, Japan has been a top destination for Chinese tourists and popular among Chinese students.
Tensions between Beijing and Tokyo escalated last month after Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi suggested Japan could deploy military forces in the event of a conflict in the Taiwan Strait.
Takaichi’s comments made her the first sitting Japanese leader since World War II to publicly link a Taiwan contingency with the possible deployment of Japan’s Self-Defence Forces. She has refused to retract the comments despite Beijing’s repeated protests.
On Thursday, Beijing dismissed Takaichi’s apparent attempt to dial down weeks of tensions when she told the Japanese parliament a day earlier that Tokyo’s position on Taiwan remained unchanged.
The Chinese foreign ministry criticised her latest remarks as “prevarication”, warning that it “absolutely does not accept” her continued refusal to retract the comments.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States and Japan, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state. But Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
With the free refund policies, hundreds of thousands of Chinese travellers have since cancelled their planned trips to Japan, leading airlines to scrap a growing number of scheduled flights.
Last week, Chinese media reports indicated that some domestic airlines cancelled flights on 12 of their routes to Japan, including popular tourism destinations Kyoto and Osaka.
Several employees of China’s state-owned enterprises said they had been advised to scrap their immediate travel plans to Japan.
In addition, Beijing has frozen youth exchange programmes with Tokyo amid the fallout, a diplomatic source said.
Russia, South Korea and some countries in Southeast Asia are now seeing a wave of bookings from Chinese tourists who would otherwise have visited Japan, according to various travel platforms.

data.title: Netflix buys Warner Bros for US$72 billion, gaining Harry Potter, Game of Thrones
data.description:
Netflix has agreed to buy Warner Bros Discovery’s TV, film studios and streaming division for US$72 billion, a deal that would hand control of one of Hollywood’s most prized and oldest assets to the streaming pioneer.
The agreement, announced on Friday, follows a weeks-long bidding war in which Netflix offered nearly US$28-a-share, eclipsing Paramount Skydance’s close to US$24 bid for the whole of Warner Bros Discovery, including the cable TV assets slated for a spin-off.
Buying the owner of marquee franchises including Game of Thrones, “DC Comics” and “Harry Potter” will further tilt the balance of power in Hollywood in favour of Netflix.
It would help the streaming giant, which has so far built its dominance without major deals or a large content library, to ward off competition from Walt Disney and the Ellison family-backed Paramount.
The two companies together will “help define the next century of storytelling”, said Netflix co-CEO Ted Sarandos, who had once said “the goal is to become HBO faster than HBO can become us”.

Antitrust scrutiny likely
The deal, however, is likely to face strong antitrust scrutiny in Europe and the US as it would give the world’s largest streaming service ownership of a rival that is home to HBO Max and boasts nearly 130 million streaming subscribers.
David Ellison-led Paramount, which kicked off the bidding war with a series of unsolicited offers and has close ties with the Trump administration, had questioned the sale process earlier this week and alleged favourable treatment to Netflix.
Even before the bids were in, some members of Congress said a Netflix–Warner Bros Discovery deal could harm consumers and Hollywood.
Cinema United, a global exhibition trade association, said on Friday the deal poses an “unprecedented threat” to cinemas worldwide.
“In light of the current regulatory environment, this will raise eyebrows and concerns. The combined dominant streaming player will be heavily scrutinised,” said PP Foresight analyst Paolo Pescatore.
“We should expect this to wrangle on given Paramount Skydance’s pursuit for Warner Bros Discovery.”
Looking to allay some concerns, Netflix said the deal would give subscribers more shows and films, boost its US production and long-term spending on original content and create more jobs and opportunities for creative talent.
The company argued in deal talks that a combination of its streaming service with HBO Max would benefit consumers by lowering the cost of a bundled offering.
The company has told Warner Bros Discovery it would keep releasing the studio’s films in cinemas in a bid to ease fears that its deal would eliminate another studio and major source of theatrical films, according to media reports.

Cash and stock deal
Warner Bros Discovery shares were up 2.4 per cent at US$25 in premarket trading, while Netflix fell nearly 3 per cent and Paramount 2.2 per cent. Comcast, the third suitor, was trading little changed.
Paramount and Comcast did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Under the deal, each Warner Bros Discovery shareholder will receive US$23.25 in cash and about US$4.50 in Netflix stock per share, valuing Warner at US$27.75 a share, or about US$72 billion in equity and US$82.7 billion, including debt.
The deal represents a premium of 121.3 per cent to Warner Bros Discovery’s closing price on September 10, before initial reports of a possible buyout emerged.
The deal is expected to close after Warner Bros Discovery spins off its global networks unit, Discovery Global, into a separate listed company, a move now set for completion in the third quarter of 2026.
Netflix has offered Warner Bros Discovery a US$5.8 billion break-up fee, while Warner Bros Discovery would pay Netflix US$2.8 billion if the deal collapses.
Netflix said it expects to generate at least US$2 billion to US$3 billion in annual cost savings by the third year, after the deal closes.

Netflix growth worries
Analysts have said Netflix is driven by a desire to lock up long-term rights to hit shows and films and rely less on outside studios as it expands into gaming and looks for new avenues of growth after the success of its password-sharing crackdown.
Its shares are up just 16 per cent this year, after surging more than 80 per cent in 2024, as investors worry its breakneck growth could be slowing, especially after it stopped disclosing subscriber figures earlier this year.
The company has leaned on its ad-supported tier to drive growth, but that is not expected to become a major revenue engine until next year, while analysts say its push into video games has stumbled amid strategy shifts and executive turnover.
Buying Warner Bros would also deepen its gaming bet, as WBD is one of the few entertainment companies to notch big successes in the sector, including its Harry Potter title Hogwarts Legacy, which has generated more than US$1 billion in revenue.

data.title: As China defies US tech blockade, can local AI chipmakers capture Nvidia’s market share?
data.description:
With the technology race between the United States and China, the world’s two largest economies, intensifying even though a trade truce was reached in late October, analysts say the prospects of China achieving self-sufficiency in semiconductors and artificial intelligence (AI) are positive, despite American efforts to maintain leads in those fields.
“If any country can do it [achieve self-sufficiency in semiconductors], it’s China, owing to its vast resources and pool of engineering talent,” Paul Triolo, a partner at DGA-Albright Stonebridge Group, was quoted as saying in a report published by Goldman Sachs on Thursday.
While the idea that the US could maintain its lead in advanced AI hinged on the assumption that scaling up computational power would guarantee superior models, Chinese firms like Alibaba and DeepSeek had demonstrated an ability to build advanced models that used far less computing power, Triolo said. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
“So, the US expanding or even maintaining its current lead is not a foregone conclusion,” he said.
A bipartisan group of US senators unveiled a bill on Thursday that would prevent the Trump administration from loosening rules that restrict Beijing’s access to advanced AI chips for two and a half years.
“The best AI chips are made by American companies,” said Republican Senator Pete Ricketts, one of the bill’s authors. “Denying Beijing access to these AI chips is essential to our national security.”
The Trump administration has vacillated on allowing the export of advanced chips to China, uncertain about whether doing so would create a dependency or boost China’s military and technology sectors.
On Wednesday, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang met US President Donald Trump to discuss US export controls, as the administration weighs up whether to permit the sale of Nvidia’s H200 processors into the Chinese market.
Huang told reporters after the meeting that he was unsure whether China would accept the H200 chips if the US relaxed restrictions. The H200 has been supplanted at the top of Nvidia’s AI chip line-up by the B200, which was unveiled in March.
“We can’t degrade chips that we sell to China, they won’t accept that,” Huang said.
From the perspective of supply chain development, China’s AI story is only wrapping up its initial chapter
Bernstein analysts
This summer, Nvidia won US approval to sell its less powerful H20 chip, which was designed specifically for the Chinese market by falling just below export thresholds. But Chinese regulators later reportedly ordered leading technology companies to halt purchases of those chips, with Beijing launching antitrust investigations into Nvidia while raising questions about security risks.
Advanced Micro Devices CEO Lisa Su said on Thursday that it had licences to sell some of its MI 308 chips to China and was prepared to pay a 15 per cent tax to the US government if it shipped them – a condition included in an agreement the Trump administration reached with both AMD and Nvidia in August.
Analysts from Bernstein said Nvidia was unlikely to resume sales in China any time soon given the Chinese government’s dedication to supporting the domestic supply chain and, as a result, local players were expected to capture more than 90 per cent of the market share by 2028.
“2025 has been a good year for China AI initiated by DeepSeek, but from the perspective of supply chain development, China’s AI story is only wrapping up its initial chapter,” they said in a report issued on Thursday.
They said the biggest bottleneck was constrained domestic advanced logic production capacity, which had limited the output of AI chips, but a critical turning point was looming and advanced logic capacity should start growing rapidly in the next two years.
“The domestic supply chain is gearing up quickly to meet this surging demand,” the report said.

data.title: 10 pictures of 10 days of Hong Kong’s Tai Po tragedy, city’s deadliest fire in decades
data.description:
As Hong Kong marks 10 days since the deadly blaze at Tai Po’s Wang Fuk Court, we have put together 10 pictures from Post photographers showing how the fire unfolded, and how Hongkongers came together to mourn those who lost their lives and help residents who lost their homes and loved ones.









Click here for more pictures documenting the Tai Po fire and the aftermath.

data.title: Baby Jesus zip-tied by ICE in ‘political’ nativity scene challenges Trump immigration policies
data.description:
Mother Mary wearing a respirator mask to protect herself from tear gas. Baby Jesus with zip-tied hands, wrapped in a thin blanket that looks like aluminium foil. Masked centurions with sunglasses and green vests labelled “ICE”.
This is how the Lake Street Church of Evanston in suburban Chicago chose to assemble its nativity scene for the Christmas season. The church and its leaders have been vocal critics of the Trump administration’s Operation Midway Blitz and expressed a message with their holiday decorations.
“Enforcement terror does not discriminate by documentation status,” the church wrote on its Facebook page on November 25.
The organiser of the nativity scene, Associate Minister Jillian Westerfield, said that in the months before organising it, she has been reflecting on the impact of Operation Midway Blitz, in which hundreds of immigrants have been detained, the overwhelming majority of whom do not have criminal histories that have a “high public safety risk”.
“For me at least, I have experienced seeing the face of Christ in the people who are suffering,” Westerfield said, pointing to arrests, detainments and altercations in Evanston and Chicago. “We just really saw a parallel between the ICE forces and the centurions.”
This is what the Bible says happened to Jesus, and we use our contemporary imagery to illustrate that
Associate Minister Jillian Westerfield
“Of course it’s political,” but the set is not up for political interpretation, Westerfield said.
“This is what the Bible says happened to Jesus, and we use our contemporary imagery to illustrate that,” she said. “I do feel like it’s a pretty straightforward representation of what the Bible says happened to Jesus’s family.”
According to Biblical texts, King Herod, fearing that a rival had been born to usurp his power, tried to kill infant Jesus by ordering the murder of all baby boys in Bethlehem. Jesus’ father Joseph acted quickly to take his wife Mary and baby Jesus and flee Bethlehem to avoid the bloodshed.
Most traditional, popular renditions of the nativity depict peaceful scenes of the newborn Jesus lying in a manger with his parents, angels, shepherds and their animals prayerfully looking on.

Westerfield said the feelings about ICE, the US Immigration and Customs Enforcement agency, are not just shared by the church’s leadership, but also its congregation. “This is who we are, and this is what’s important to us. And we would be much more afraid to hold back, because ultimately, we feel like we answer to God, not to anyone else,” Westerfield said.
Outside the reaction from the church’s congregants, Westerfield said there were always concerns about reactions from outside forces, but that they will not let themselves cower from expressing themselves. If someone is really upset by the nativity set, “I hope that what happens is they examine why they’re upset and maybe find within their own conscience a change of heart,” she said.
Reverend Michael Woolf, a frequent protester of Operation Midway Blitz and the lead pastor for the Lake Street Church, said: “Christians have to look at the birth story – not just a sort of a rosy sort of tale that we can just read in scripture – but actually sort of wrestle with its coming into being in context.”

“We don’t speak for all Christians, but we certainly speak for a certain strand of community that’s trying to take that message and say, ‘If Jesus were born in America right now, what would this nativity set look like?’” Woolf said.
Lake Street Church and Westerfield previously organised nativity sets with political messages. In 2023, the church organised a nativity set with only Baby Jesus surrounded by rubble, as an homage to the people trapped in the war in Gaza.
“We were all very, and still are of course, very concerned about the situation in Gaza, and the suffering of people all over the world,” Westerfield said. The reception the church received then was largely positive, she said. The church also held an outdoor prayer vigil for Palestine then.
